questions.question: What does the term "behaviour that challenges" actually describe?
options: A medical diagnosis given by a doctor
A difficult or aggressive type of person
An action and its impact, not a person's character
A permanent label that stays in a person's records

questions.question: Understood correctly, behaviour that challenges is almost always a sign of what?
options: A character flaw that cannot be changed
A person seeking attention for no reason
Deliberate rule-breaking
Distress and an attempt to communicate an unmet need

questions.question: Why do we avoid labelling someone as "aggressive" or "attention-seeking"?
options: It changes how the team treats them and stops us asking what they are trying to tell us
Because such labels are always factually inaccurate
It makes it harder to fill in incident forms
Because the care plan forbids writing anything about behaviour

questions.question: When someone cannot easily tell us what is wrong, what does their behaviour become?
options: Their language, a way of communicating a need
A sign that they should be left alone
A problem to be controlled
Proof that they are being difficult on purpose

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a physical discomfort that can lead to distress?
options: Too much noise and bustle around them
Needing the toilet or being in pain
Feeling lonely or ignored
Having too little to do

questions.question: Which of these is an example of an emotional unmet need?
options: Boredom or feeling ignored
Feeling too hot or too cold
Thirst
Constipation

questions.question: In this course, what does "over-stimulation" refer to?
options: Too much noise and bustle in the environment
A side effect of medication
Having too little to do
A physical illness

questions.question: Behaviour that challenges is more likely among which group of people?
options: People who have chosen not to cooperate
Only people who are naturally aggressive
People who find it hard to communicate or make sense of what is happening
Only people living with dementia

questions.question: According to the course, what is your role as a care worker when someone shows behaviour that challenges?
options: To keep your distance and avoid them
To understand and support them, and meet the need behind the behaviour
To control and manage the person
To insist they follow the rules of the home

questions.question: What is a "trigger"?
options: A stage of recovery after a crisis
A written record of an incident
A technique for physical intervention
Something that sets off distress

questions.question: Which of the following can make personal care itself a trigger for distress?
options: Working at the person's pace
Explaining each step before you do it
Being rushed or approached from behind without explanation
Offering the person choices

questions.question: You need to help someone with personal care who has become distressed during it before. Which approach is most likely to reduce that risk?
options: Do the care in silence so as not to draw attention to it
Approach quietly from behind so they do not notice
Get it over with as quickly as possible
Slow down, explain each step, offer choices and work at their pace

questions.question: In the escalation cycle, what does "baseline" describe?
options: The moment distress peaks
The person calm and settled, their usual self
The period of recovery after a crisis
The early signs of anxiety after a trigger

questions.question: At which stage of the escalation cycle is calm support most effective?
options: The build-up stage, when early signs appear
During recovery, once distress has passed
At crisis, when distress has peaked
There is no stage where support helps

questions.question: Which of these is an early warning sign shown in a person's voice?
options: Becoming louder, faster or quieter, or repeating the same words
A tense posture
Pacing or restlessness
Clenched fists

questions.question: Why does getting to know a person as an individual matter so much?
options: Because what looks like agitation in one person may be their normal way of being
Because it lets you predict exactly how everyone will behave
Because it means you never have to follow the care plan
Because it removes the need to keep any records

questions.question: What is good practice when writing records about a person's distress?
options: Only record something once it has gone badly wrong
Keep the details to yourself
Record your opinions about their character
Write facts: what you saw and heard, when it happened and what helped

questions.question: Why is staying calm yourself the single most helpful thing you can do?
options: Because it proves the behaviour is not your fault
Because it means you can finish the task faster
Because it shows the person you are in charge
Because distress and calm are both contagious, and your calm helps the person feel safe

questions.question: Which of these is recommended body language when supporting a distressed person?
options: Standing over them to show authority
Making sudden movements to get their attention
Blocking their way so they cannot move
Staying at or below their eye level with an open, relaxed posture

questions.question: What does active listening involve when someone is distressed?
options: Explaining why their feelings are mistaken
Correcting them so they see the situation clearly
Giving full attention and acknowledging feelings, without arguing
Filling every pause so there is no silence

questions.question: How can you give a distressed person space and time to settle?
options: Insist they calm down quickly
Fire questions at them until they answer
Gather colleagues around to watch
Step back, lower your voice, allow silence and reduce the audience

questions.question: What is the best way to offer choices to reduce distress?
options: Make a firm demand rather than a choice
Offer as many options as possible at once
Offer two clear, simple options and respect the person's answer
Decide for the person to save time

questions.question: If you can see what is causing a person's distress, what is often the quickest way to help?
options: Tell the person to ignore it
Change or remove the trigger, for example turning down noise or pausing an upsetting task
Wait for the crisis to pass before doing anything
Carry on with the task regardless

questions.question: What does a behaviour support plan set out?
options: A record of complaints about the person
A list of punishments for behaviour
Instructions for physical restraint
The person's known triggers, early signs and the approaches that work best for them

questions.question: What is a dynamic risk assessment?
options: A way of thinking that reads a situation moment by moment and adjusts your response
A form you fill in once a year
A fixed set of rules that never changes
A technique for restraining a person safely

questions.question: The phrase "behaviour that challenges" is a label for a person's difficult or aggressive character.
options: True
False

questions.question: Every behaviour has a meaning and serves a purpose for the person, even when the reason is not obvious.
options: True
False

questions.question: Distress usually appears all at once rather than building up in stages.
options: True
False

questions.question: Once a person has reached crisis, there is far less you can do except keep everyone safe and wait for the distress to pass.
options: True
False

questions.question: Labelling someone as "a challenge" helps the whole team support them better.
options: True
False

questions.question: Withdrawing to a safe distance when a situation becomes unsafe is a sound decision, not a failure.
options: True
False

questions.question: Physical intervention is an acceptable routine way of managing a person's behaviour.
options: True
False

questions.question: Any physical intervention that is used must always be recorded and reported.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are examples of physical discomfort that can lead to distress? (Select all that apply)
options: Needing the toilet
Feeling ignored
Feeling too hot or too cold
Boredom
Pain

questions.question: Which of these are environmental triggers described in the course? (Select all that apply)
options: Hunger
Pain or illness
Loud noise
Unfamiliar places
Bright lights

questions.question: Which of these are early warning signs that a person's distress is rising? (Select all that apply)
options: Pacing or restlessness
Repeating the same words or questions
Clenched fists or a tense posture
A relaxed, open expression
Being calm and settled as usual

questions.question: Which of these help to give a distressed person space and time? (Select all that apply)
options: Gently moving others away so they are not watched by a crowd
Allowing silence without rushing to fill every pause
Insisting they calm down quickly
Stepping back a little and lowering your voice
Firing questions to make them respond

questions.question: Which groups of people are more likely to show behaviour that challenges? (Select all that apply)
options: People living with dementia
Only people who are naturally aggressive
People with a learning disability or autism
People with a mental illness such as anxiety, depression or psychosis

questions.question: Which of these are good practice when keeping records of a person's distress? (Select all that apply)
options: Share and update the care plan so everyone responds the same way
Write facts, not opinions
Record opinions in order to blame the person
Look for patterns over time, such as a certain time of day

questions.question: Which principles must be followed whenever any restriction is placed on a person? (Select all that apply)
options: Apply it for the shortest time necessary
Use it whenever it makes the shift easier for staff
Make it proportionate to the actual risk
Use the least restrictive option that keeps people safe

questions.question: Understood correctly, behaviour that challenges is almost always a sign of ___ and an attempt to communicate a need that has not been met.
The build-up stage is your best opportunity to help; the sooner you notice a person's early warning ___, the sooner you can meet the need.
Positive behaviour support puts most of its effort into ___ rather than waiting for behaviour to occur and reacting to it.
Physically intervening is only ever a ___ resort, used when there is a real and immediate risk of serious harm and everything else has failed.
Keeping people safe means constantly weighing up the risk as a situation unfolds, which is called a ___ risk assessment.
